Paragraph

Suppose that \(\{ c_{k}\}\) is a sequence in \(C[a, b]\text{,}\) Cauchy in \(L^{p}[a, b]\)-norm, representing \(\phi\) such that \(\Vert c_{k}^{-} \Vert_{L^{p}}\to 0\) as \(k\to \infty\text{.}\) Define \(c_{k}^{+}=c_{k}- c_{k}^{-}\text{,}\) which is identified as \(\max\{ c_{k}(x), 0\}\text{.}\) Then \(\{ c_{k}^{+}\}\) is a sequence in \(C[a, b]\text{,}\) Cauchy in \(L^{p}[a, b]\)-norm. Proposition 2.8 applied to both \(\{ c_{k}^{+}\}\) and \(\{ c_{k}^{-}\}\) implies that there exists a subsequence, still indexed by \(k\) to avoid extra indices, such that \(\lim_{k\to \infty} c_{k}^{+}(x)\) exists \(a.e.\text{,}\) which is evidently \(\ge 0\) \(a.e.\text{,}\) and \(\lim_{k\to \infty} c_{k}^{-}(x)\to 0\) \(a.e.\) (refer to (ii) of Proposition 2.8 ). Therefore
\begin{equation*} \lim_{k\to \infty} c_{k}(x)= \lim_{k\to \infty} c_{k}^{+}(x) + \lim_{k\to \infty} c_{k}^{-}(x)\ge 0 \; a.e. \end{equation*}
in-context
